section.title: Knowing God by Obedience
section.sectionRationale: John gives the first great test of true faith: we can be sure we know God if we keep His commandments and walk as Jesus walked. He shows that the old command to love is also new in Christ, that hating a brother is to remain in darkness, and he reassures his readers by their standing in Christ.
section.thesis: Assurance that we truly know God is confirmed by keeping His commandments and walking as Jesus walked, and above all by loving our brothers, for the one who hates walks in darkness while the one who loves remains in the light in which Christ has already secured them.
section.frameNote: Three beats: knowing God confirmed by obedience; the old yet new command to love, with light against the darkness of hatred; and words of assurance to children, fathers, and young men.

units.label: Knowing Him by keeping His commandments

verses.text: By this we can be sure that we have come to {{ginosko|know}} Him: if we {{tereo|keep}} His {{entole|commandments}}.
If anyone says, “I know Him,” but does not keep His commandments, he is a liar, and the truth is not in him.
But if anyone keeps His word, the {{agape|love}} of God has been truly {{teleioo|perfected}} in him. By this we know that we are in Him:
Whoever claims to {{meno|abide}} in Him must walk as Jesus walked.
Beloved, I am not writing to you a new commandment, but an old one, which you have had from the beginning. This commandment is the message you have heard.
Then again, I am also writing to you a new commandment, which is true in Him and also in you. For the darkness is fading and the true light is already shining.
If anyone claims to be in the light but hates his brother, he is still in the darkness.
Whoever loves his brother remains in the light, and there is no cause of stumbling in him.
But whoever hates his brother is in the darkness and walks in the darkness. He does not know where he is going, because the darkness has blinded his eyes.

units.label: Words of assurance
units.note: to children, fathers, and young men

verses.text: I am writing to you, little children, because your sins have been forgiven through His name.

I am writing to you, fathers, because you know Him who is from the beginning. I am writing to you, young men, because you have {{nikao|overcome}} the evil one. I have written to you, children, because you know the Father.

I have written to you, fathers, because you know Him who is from the beginning. I have written to you, young men, because you are strong, and the word of God abides in you, and you have overcome the evil one.

ginosko.gloss: To know, often by experience and relationship, not merely by information. To know God in this sense is to be in living fellowship with Him. John says this real knowing shows itself in a life of obedience; a claim to know God without obedience is empty.

tereo.gloss: To keep, guard, observe, hold fast. To keep His commandments is not cold rule-keeping but treasuring and obeying what God has said out of love. It is the fruit and evidence of truly knowing Him, not the means of earning His favour.

entole.gloss: Commandment, instruction, charge. In this letter the commandments centre on believing in Christ and loving one another. Keeping them is the mark of those who know God, and John will show that love is the heart of what God commands.

agape.gloss: Love, especially self-giving love that seeks the good of another. The love of God can mean His love for us, our love for Him, or both together; it is brought to its goal, perfected, in the one who keeps His word. This love is the pulse of the whole letter.

teleioo.gloss: To complete, bring to its goal, make perfect in the sense of mature and fully realised. When we keep God's word, His love reaches its intended end in us. It speaks of love coming to full expression, not of sinless flawlessness.

meno.gloss: To remain, abide, dwell, continue. A key word in John for the settled, ongoing union between the believer and Christ. To abide in Him is to stay in living fellowship with Him, and the proof of that claim is walking as Jesus walked.

nikao.gloss: To conquer, overcome, be victorious. John tells the young men they have overcome the evil one because the word of God abides in them. The victory is real and already won in Christ, giving grounds for assurance in the fight against sin and error.

basic.context: How can you know you really know God? John gives a plain test: those who truly know Him keep His commandments and live the way Jesus lived. He is not talking about earning God's love but about the fruit that proves it is real. The command he keeps returning to is love. To claim to be in the light while hating a fellow believer is to be in the dark. Then he reassures his readers with what is already true of them in Christ.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a test of assurance, not a ladder to salvation. Obedience and love are the evidence that we have come to know God, not the price of knowing Him. John writes tenderly, calling his readers little children, fathers, and young men, and reminding them their sins are forgiven and the evil one is overcome, so the test builds confidence rather than crushing it.
basic.originalLanguages: To know, ginosko, God is to be in living fellowship with Him, shown by keeping, tereo, His commandments, entole. When we keep His word the love, agape, of God is perfected, teleioo, brought to its goal, in us. To abide, meno, in Him is to walk as Jesus walked, and John assures the young men they have overcome, nikao, the evil one.
basic.typology: The old commandment given at the beginning and made new in Christ echoes the law given to Israel, now fulfilled and deepened in Jesus, who loved to the end. The true light already shining is the dawn of the promised new age in Christ, breaking on the fading darkness.
basic.christ: Jesus is the pattern, whoever abides in Him must walk as He walked, and He is the substance of the command, the love that fulfils the law. He is the true light already shining, and it is through His name that sins are forgiven, the ground of all the assurance John gives.
basic.god: God is knowable, and He is known truly in a life of obedient love. He is the God whose love comes to its goal in His people, who forgives sins through His Son's name, and who is called both the One from the beginning and the Father, drawing His children into secure fellowship.
basic.crossReferences: John 13:34 gives the new commandment to love as Jesus loved. John 14:15 says if you love Me you will keep My commandments. John 15 speaks of abiding in Christ, and Leviticus 19:18 is the old command to love your neighbour.
basic.oneStory: The old command to love, first given long ago, becomes new in Jesus, who kept it perfectly and pours His love into His people. As the true light dawns, God's children walk as Jesus walked and love one another, living proof that they have come to know the God the whole story reveals.

context.literary: A tightly woven section using 'By this we know' as a refrain of assurance. It moves from the test of obedience, to the old yet new command to love set against the darkness of hatred, to a poetic address to three groups, repeated for emphasis, grounding the tests in what is already theirs in Christ.
context.historical: The separatist teachers apparently claimed a superior knowledge of God while showing little regard for obedience or for fellow believers. John insists that true knowledge of God is inseparable from keeping His commandments and loving the brothers, exposing their claims as empty.
context.canonical: The passage stands on Jesus' own new commandment in John 13 and 15 and on the ancient command of Leviticus 19:18, and it anticipates the fuller treatment of love in 1 John 3 and 4. Knowing God by obedience echoes the covenant logic of the whole Bible.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give the readers a reliable test of whether they truly know God, obedience and love, to expose the emptiness of claiming to know God while hating a brother, and to reassure believers of their secure standing in Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage both describes the mark of those who know God, obedience and love, and prescribes it, keep His commandments, walk as Jesus walked, love your brother. The assurances of verses 12 to 14 describe what is already true of believers in Christ.

debates.question: Does 'we know we have come to know Him if we keep His commandments' make obedience the basis of salvation?

views.label: Obedience is the evidence, not the basis, of knowing God
views.case: John writes to give assurance, 'by this we can be sure', so obedience functions as the fruit that confirms a real relationship, not the payment that secures it. The letter grounds forgiveness in Christ's name and atoning work, and presents love and obedience as what flows from truly knowing God. This is the strongest reading and it fits the whole New Testament, where good works are the fruit of grace.

views.label: Obedience earns or maintains our standing with God
views.case: Some read the test as making our acceptance depend on our performance; but this contradicts the letter's assurance that our sins are already forgiven through His name and that Jesus is our advocate.

views.label: Obedience is optional for those who claim to know God
views.case: The false teachers effectively said this, claiming knowledge without obedience; John flatly calls such a person a liar.

debates.bearing: The strongest orthodox reading takes obedience and love as the God-given evidence that we truly know Him, giving assurance to the humble and exposing empty claims, while resting salvation itself on Christ's finished work and His advocacy.

debates.question: What is 'the love of God' that is perfected when we keep His word?

views.label: God's love reaching its goal in us as we obey
views.case: The phrase most naturally speaks of God's own love coming to its intended end in the believer, expressed in a life of obedience and love for others. This fits John's later statement that His love is perfected in us when we love one another, 1 John 4:12.

views.label: Our love for God brought to maturity
views.case: Others read it as the believer's love for God growing to full expression through obedience, which is a true and related sense.

views.label: A deliberate both-and
views.case: Many hold that John means God's love received and returned, a single flow that comes to its goal as we keep His word; the Greek allows the richness.

debates.bearing: However the phrase is weighted, the point stands: keeping God's word is where His love comes to full and visible expression, so a claim to love God that ignores His commandments is empty, and genuine obedience is love brought to its goal.

study.originalLanguages: John's test turns on words of relationship and evidence. To know, ginosko, God is to share living fellowship with Him, and the sure sign is that we keep, tereo, His commandments, entole, treasuring and obeying His word rather than merely knowing about it. In the one who keeps His word, the agape, the love of God, is teleioo, brought to its goal and full expression. To claim to meno, to abide in Him, is to walk as Jesus walked. And to the young men John says they have already nikao, overcome, conquered the evil one, because the word of God abides in them, so the whole test rests on grace already given.

typology.type: The old commandment from the beginning made new in Christ
typology.fulfillment: The command to love, given in the law, Leviticus 19:18, and made new by Jesus who loved to the end and commands His own to love as He loved, John 13:34.

typology.type: The true light already shining as darkness fades
typology.fulfillment: The dawn of the new age in Christ, the light of the world, John 8:12, breaking on a world still in shadow.

study.christ: Christ is both the pattern and the power of the obedient life: whoever abides in Him must walk as He walked, and He is the love that fulfils the command. He is the true light already shining, and forgiveness comes through His name, which is why John can reassure even the youngest believers. To know God is, in the end, to know and follow Jesus, and to love as He loved.
study.god: God is not unknowable or distant; He can be truly known, and that knowing shows itself in obedient love. He is the God whose love comes to its goal in His people, who forgives sins through His Son's name, and who is named both the One from the beginning and the Father. He gives real victory over the evil one, grounding the assurance of His children.

crossReferences.note: If you love Me, you will keep My commandments, tying love for Christ to obedience as John does.
The new commandment to love one another as Jesus loved, the old-yet-new command John draws on.
Love your neighbour as yourself, the ancient command that stands behind the old commandment John recalls.
If we love one another, God's love is perfected in us, echoing the perfecting of God's love here.

study.oneStory: The command to love, first spoken to God's ancient people, is taken up and made new by Jesus, who kept it perfectly and pours His love into all who abide in Him. As the true light dawns, God's children walk as Jesus walked and love one another. This obedient love is not the price of knowing God but the proof of it, the shape that fellowship with the God of the whole story takes in the world.

sermon.title: Keeping His Commands
sermon.why: Begg preaches this passage, 1 John 2:3-11, on the test of truly knowing God by keeping His commandments and loving the brothers, walking as Jesus walked. A clear, orthodox handling of assurance and obedience.

quiz.q: How can we be sure we have come to know God, according to verse 3?

options.text: By keeping His commandments as the evidence of a real relationship.
options.note: Obedience is the fruit that confirms true knowledge of God, not the price of it.

options.text: By feeling deeply religious most of the time.
options.note: Feelings can accompany faith, but the test John gives is obedience.

options.text: By earning God's acceptance through flawless performance.
options.note: Obedience is evidence, not the basis; forgiveness rests on Christ's name and work.

options.text: By gaining a superior, hidden knowledge of spiritual things.
options.note: John opposes claims to secret knowledge; the mark is obedient love, not esoteric insight.

quiz.q: What does John say about anyone who claims to know God but does not keep His commandments?

options.text: He is a liar, and the truth is not in him.
options.note: Verse 4 flatly exposes a claim to know God that is contradicted by disobedience.

options.text: He is simply a weaker but genuine believer.
options.note: John's language is stronger; the settled claim without obedience is false.

options.text: He knows God in a different but equally valid way.
options.note: John allows no valid knowledge of God divorced from obedience.

options.text: He needs only more information to believe rightly.
options.note: The problem is not lack of information but a claim contradicted by life.

quiz.q: Why does John call the command both old and new?

options.text: It is old, given from the beginning, yet made new and fulfilled in Christ as the light dawns.
options.note: Verses 7 and 8: the ancient command to love is renewed in Jesus who loved to the end.

options.text: It is old and therefore no longer binding on believers.
options.note: John does not set it aside; he presses it as the living command to love.

options.text: It is entirely new, with no roots in earlier revelation.
options.note: He calls it old too, rooted from the beginning; both are true, but this half misses the old.

options.text: It is a rule that has nothing to do with Jesus.
options.note: It is true in Him and shaped by His love; it is deeply tied to Jesus.

quiz.q: What does hating a fellow believer reveal, according to verses 9 to 11?

options.text: That the person is still in the darkness, however much they claim the light.
options.note: Hatred exposes a claim to be in the light as false; the hater walks blind in darkness.

options.text: That the person is in the light but struggling.
options.note: John says such a person is still in darkness, not merely struggling in the light.

options.text: That love and hatred make no real difference.
options.note: The whole passage makes love the decisive mark of walking in the light.

options.text: That the person simply needs to try to be nicer.
options.note: The issue is deeper than niceness; hatred reveals a heart still in darkness.

quiz.q: Why does John remind his readers of what is already true of them in verses 12 to 14?

options.text: To reassure them: their sins are forgiven and they have overcome the evil one in Christ.
options.note: The tests are set within strong assurance grounded in Christ's finished work.

options.text: To make them anxious about whether they measure up.
options.note: The tone is assurance, not anxiety; he reminds them of grace already given.

options.text: To flatter them into ignoring their sin.
options.note: John never excuses sin; he grounds real assurance in Christ, not flattery.

options.text: To single out only the mature as true believers.
options.note: He addresses children, fathers, and young men alike; assurance is for all, not only the mature.

questions: John says we can be sure we know God if we keep His commandments. How do you tell the difference between obedience as evidence of grace and obedience as an attempt to earn it?
To abide in Christ is to walk as Jesus walked. In what part of your life this week would walking as Jesus walked look most different from how you are living now?
The command John keeps returning to is love. Is there a fellow believer you find hard to love, and what would it mean to step out of that darkness into the light?
John reassures his readers that their sins are forgiven and the evil one is overcome. How does resting in what is already true of you in Christ fuel obedience rather than replace it?
The true light is already shining and the darkness is fading. Where do you see that light breaking into your own habits, relationships, and desires?

translationNotes.note: 'The love of God' is a genitive that can mean God's love for us, our love for God, or both; John's usage across the letter suggests God's love coming to its goal in the obedient believer.
'The darkness is fading and the true light is already shining' uses present tenses to picture the overlap of the ages, the new age dawning in Christ while the old darkness passes.

The shift from 'I am writing' to 'I have written' in verses 13 and 14 is likely stylistic emphasis rather than a change of meaning; the BSB footnote notes 'overcome the evil one' can read 'overcome evil'.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 John 2:3-14. Verse 13 line breaks joined with spaces; no em dashes in this passage. Footnote on 2:13 ('overcome evil') noted.
